This is from a database I put together for my Phaserip game, I''m not going to try and do this all in one go, but by categories...

AXES
Developed as tools but weaponized rapidly in prehistoric times, axes are characterized as any short staff upon which is mounted a stone or metal head, typically affixed at a right angle, and featuring a sharpened cutting edge.


Axe-hammer
A characteristically shaped perforated stone axe used in the Neolithic and
Bronze Ages, with thungsten often added for weight and wood handles up to 6' in length.


Battle-axe
Specialized versions of utility axes designed for warfare, ranging in size from 1 to 5' (30 cm - 1.5m) long and weighing 1 to 6 lbs (0.5kg - 3 kg). Later variations added a sharp, curved pick to the rear of the blade, or a stabbing spike atop. Battle Axes fell out of favour with mounted knights between 1100 and 1400, superseded by long straight swords designed for slashing which were devastating against the boiled-leather armour of foot-soldiers and capable of precise attacks at the weak spots of maille-covered knights. With the development of full-plate armour in the 15th c., however, hafted weapons with greater impact-power were called for and the battle-axe was capable of slicing through an opponent's armour by concentrating the weight of its single, sharpened, crescent-shaped wedge on a small target area of metal plate.


*Bearded Axe (Skeggöx, O.N. Skegg, "beard" + öx, "axe") -
various axes used as toolsand weapons as early as the 6th c., primarily
associated with Viking Age Scandinavians.The "beard", or lower part of
the axe's bit, extends below the width of the butt to provide a wide cutting
surface w/o excess weight, used in battle to hook onto weapons & shields.


*Dane Axe (Danish axe, English long axe, hafted axe) - early type
of battle-axe primarily used during the Viking Age and early Middle Ages,
characterized by a light and thin (2mm) blade constructed with a reinforced bit
made of high carbon steel. Proportionally, a Dane axe is closer in relation to a
cleaver than a wood axe, featuring an ash or oak shaft 3 to 4' long (0.9 -
1.2m), and weighing between 2 and 4 lbs (1kg - 2 kg).


*Horseman's Axe - battle-axe tailored for the use of mounted
knights, avg. 27" (69 cm) in length, with a belt hook for ease of carrying, a hole
on the haft for the accommodation of a leather wrist-strap, and langets. Rather
than a pick, it was often fitted with a hammer face: opposite the axe blade; for
crushing blows.


* Lábrys ( Roman bipennis) - Scandinavian symmetrical double-
headed axe originating in ancient Crete and one of the oldest symbols of
Greek civilization.


*Ono - Japanese axe, up to 6' in length, the large head featuring a
very convex cutting edge with a scroll-shaped peen opposite. Particularly
associated with the sōhei or yamabushi (warrior-monks).


*Palstave - Bronze axe popular in Bronze Age Europe, hafted by
means of a forked wooden handle secured with high, cast flanges & a stop bar.


*Parashu - Indian battle-axe, typically wielded two-handed.


*Sagaris - Persian battle-axe used by the horse-riding Steppe
Eurasians, Scythians, and, according to Aristarchus of Samothrace, the
legendary Amazons. It was long-shafted with a metal head, either a sharp
(axe-like) or blunt (hammer-like) edge on one side and a sharp, straight or
curving, 'ice pick'-like point opposite.
Klevets - a shorter form of sagaris.



.

PICK/ PICKAXE
An axe-like tool consisting of a long handle set at a right angle in the middle of a curved iron bar with a point at one end & point or chisel at the other, designed for breaking up hard rocks. Picks were used as weapons by a number of ancient cultures and found new favour in the late Middle Ages for their effectiveness against steel plate.


Shepherd's Axe (Czech, valaška; Hungarian, fokos; Romanian, baltag)
A long, thin, light axe combining the features of a tool with a walking stck that could also be used as a light weapon. The shaft is usually longer than a meter and the headpiece; sharp on one side and flat on the other so it could be used as a makeshift hammer; formed to fit comfortably in the hand while walking. Often the shaft is elaborately carved by the owner.


Sickle (brishing hook, bagging hook, grasshook, rip-hook, swap hook) *
A short-handled tool with a curved blade, the edge on the inside of blade's curve. Like other farming tools, sickles lend themselves naturally as improvised blade weapons and weaponized variations are common worldwide.


* Falx (Lat. "sickle")
Bronze-age two-handed sickle-like weapon used by the Thracians and Dacians, consisting of a 3' (.91 m) wooden shaft with a curved iron blade of nearly equal length; sharpened on the inside edge like that of a scythe. It was said capable of splitting a helmet or shield in two with a single blow. The falx forced the only documented change in Roman armour brought about by an encounter with a new weapon: adding extra reinforcing bars to their helmets after first encountering the falx in Dacia.
Sica - Dacian one-handed variation of the falx, with a blade around 16" (42 cm) long and a handle roughly 1/3 longer than the blade.


Rhomphaia
Thracian weapon in use as early as 400 BC, similar to the falx, but less drastically curved. The blade is approx. 24 - 31.5" (60 - 80 cm) with a hilt of similar length.


*Jī Zhuǎ lián (chicken sickles, Chinese, lit. "chicken-claw sickle") -
bladed weapons similar to the hook sword and Okinawan kama that can be used as a single or double weapon. Constructed from a piece of metal resembling the claw of a chicken combined with a spearhead,mounted on a 1.5' length of stick. Considered a special weapon of the Xinyi Liuhe style.
Jī Dāo lián (Chinese, lit."chicken-sabre sickle") - variation that features a sword- blade instead of a spearhead, somewhat resembling a one-handed pole-arm.


*Kama - Traditional Filipino and Japanese farming implement similar to a small scythe, designed for reaping crops but frequently employed as a weapon. Use of kama is often taught in schools of karate, silat, and some Chinese martial arts.


*Knob-sickle (German, Knopfsichel) - Bronze-age sickle named for a protruding knob at the base of the blade.

CLUBS
Essentially a heavy stick used to bludgeon opponents, and the term is used loosely here to cover several variations on this theme.


Cosh (blackjack)
Short, leather-covered, typically lead-filled clu, often no more than a foot (30.5 cm) in length, often used to knock an opponent unconscious.
Slapjack - variation of the cosh consisting of a longer strap which lets it be used flail-like.


Cudgel (singlestick)
The most basic form of club, a cudgel refers to any heavy stick with a thick end, used as a weapon since prehistoric times. The term cudgel comes from the Middle Ages, where clubs were used as both walking sticks and weapons, and regiments of Clubmen were raised as late as the English Civil War.


*Knobkierrie (knobkerry; Dutch, knop; Zulu, iwisa) - short, strong wooden club with a heavy rounded knob on one end, commonly used among the tribes of Africa. The head/knob is often ornately carved with faces or shapes holding symbolic meanings.


*Shillelagh - Irish walking stick and club or cudgel made from a stout knotty stick with a large knob at the top, traditionally of blackthorn wood or oak. The wood was smeared with butter and placed up a chimney to cure, giving the shillelagh its characteristic shiny black appearance. They commonly weigh roughly 2 lbs (1kg) and are the length of a walking stick (distance from the floor to one's wrist with the elbow slightly bent). Many also feature a wrist strap. A variation known as a "loaded stick" is hollowed at the heavy "hitting" end and filled with molten lead. The shillelagh was originally used for settling disputes in a gentlemanly manner, and modern practitioners of the Irish martial art batareacht train in the use of the shillelagh for self-sefense.


*Nulla-nulla - short, curved hardwood club, used for hunting and tribal fighting by the Australian Aborignals.


*Oslop - extremely heavy two-handed Russian club, often iron-shod.

MACE
A class of club-like weapons that use a heavy head on the end of a handle to deliver powerful blows, typically consisting of a strong wood or metal shaft with a head of stone,copper, bronze, iron or steel. First developed during the Upper Paleolithic from the club by adding sharp spikes of flint or obsidian, the first variations were disc maces, with oddly-formed stones mounted perpendicular to their handle, known in predynastic Egypt. In the Naqada II period (3600 - 3100 BC), it was super-ceded by the piriform mace with rounded pear-form of head, with similar maceheads found in Mesopotamia circa 2450 - 1900 BC. Shardanas maces, named for the Sardinian warriors who fought for Ramses II against the Hittites, were wooden sticks with bronze heads. Unique maces called Gada were used extensively in ancient Indian warfare. While the Romans eschewed maces as heavy weapons with a wide-swinging arc were impractical in the well-discliplined tight formations of the Roman infantry, the Persions made extensive use of a variety of maces. Maces of medieval European foot soldiers were usually quite short (2 - 3' or 70 - 90 cm), while cavalry maces were longer, with the two-handed mace the longest. Eastern European maces were often of the piriform type and were especially popular in medieval Poland and the Ukraine.


*Flanged Mace - Type of mace with a head possessing protruding edges of metal, flanges, allowing it to dent or even penetrate the thickest armour plate. Though first developed c 900 in the Byzantine empire (bardoukion), the flanged mace did not achieve widespread use in Europe until the 12th c., significantly after knobbed maces.


Pernach - variation of flanged mace developed in 8th c. BC in te region of Kievan Rus', the name comes from the Ukrainian pero ("feather"), referencing how the head resembles a fletched arrow. Called a shestopyor mace in Britain.


*Roundhead - Type of mace used by the Cavaliers during the English Civil War, with a head of 9" (23 cm), a staff 6' (1.8 m) long inserted into the head, 12 iron spikes round about, with another spike on the end.


Mere
A traditional hand weapon of the Māori, shaped like an enlarged tear drop and used to strike/jab an opponent. The mere is a short club, spatulate and leaf-shaped, with a broad, rounded apex narrowing to form a handle and terminating in a butt or heel marked by several grooves. Made from Nephrite jade, it has two convex, almost flat sides like a paddle, with a rounded top often ground to a sharp edge, and is between 10 to 20" (25 - 50 cm), with an average of 14" (35 cm), long, and from under 3 up to 5" (7 - 12 cm) in width. Generally, meres have holes carved or drilled through the butt end of the handle, allowing a wrist cord to be passed through and attached to the wielder.


Kotiate - A traditional Māori hand weapon, a short club made of wood or whale-bone, named for and shaped in resemblance of the lobed part of the human liver.


Morningstar
Medieval spiked variation of the mace, usually consisting of a long spike extending straight from the top and many smaller spikes circling the head. As the mace transitioned into being constructed entirely of metal, the morningstar retained its characteristic wooden shaft. The longer two-handed form was typically 6' (1.8 m) in length. The morningstar first came into widespread use in the 14th c., particularly in Germany, where it was known as a Morgenstern. It is frequently confused with the military flail.


*Kanabō - spiked or knobbed morningstar mace used by Samurai and their retainers in feudal Japan, of varying size and a tapered shape similar to a baseball bat, constructed either of wood with iron tips, spikes, or sometimes of solid iron. Kanabōs were sometimes used in combat to smash the legs of warhorses. A cumbersome weapon, it requires great skill to recover from a miss, potentially leaving the wielder open to counterattacks. The art of using the kanabō, consisting of a mastery of both balance and strength, is called tetsubo-jutsu. In mythology, it is the favoured weapon of the oni, leading to the Japanese aphorism "like giving a kanabō to an oni", with a meaning similar to the English "adding fuel to the fire".


Plançon (plançon à picot, planson)
Medieval infantry weapon designed for smashing and thrusting, consisting of a stout iron-shod baseball bat-like 40 - 60" (1 - 1.5 m) shaft with a steel spike attached atop.


Truncheon (baton, nightstick)
Short, thick stick or baton notably carried by police oficers. These were of wood, wood- tipped with iron.
*Kobutan - short, thin, lightweight truncheon used by law enforcement officers, generally to apply pressure against certain points on the body to induce compliance without injury.
*Life Preserver - a short, weighted club carried for self-defense. Mentioned in several Sherlock Holmes stories and The Pirates of Penzance.
*Tekkan (Jap. "iron sword") - An iron truncheon closely resembling a wakizashi with a blunt iron blade, popular during the Edo period until the beginning of the 20th c.
*Telescopic Baton (Hungary, vipera) - Rigid truncheons capable of collapsing to shorter length for greater concealability. Illegal in many countries.

WARHAMMER
Late medieval close combat weapon consisting of a handle and a head designed to resemble a hammer in form and function, developed as a consequence of the prevalent use of wrought iron plate armour in the 14th and 15th c. that sword and axe blades tended to ricochet against. Warhammers could damage without penetrating the armour, transmitting the impact through even the thickest helmet to cause concussions. The handle is of various lengths, the longest equivalent to the halberd and meant for use against riders, whereas the shortest is about the same as a mace.


*Chinese Hammer (chúi, lit. "hammer") - Chinese melee weapon consisting of a large, solid metal sphere on the end of a medium-long handle. Almost always used in pairs. Relying on brute force of impact, it requires considerable strength to heft and rarely trained in by martial artists.
Liuxingchúi- variation of the chúi consisting of a smaller pair linked together by a long rope, used as a retrievable thrown weapon.


*Maul (Fr. maillet) - 14th c. long-handled warhammer with a heavy lead or iron head, similar in appearance and function to a sledgehammer, but sometimes featuring a spear-like spike on the fore-end of the haft.


*Ōtsuchi - large wooden war mallet used by the samurai class of feudal Japan, designed for forcible entry through doors and gates.

FIST WEAPONS


Bagh Nakh / Wagh Nakh ("tiger claws")
Indian weapon consisting of 4 or 5 curved blades affixed to a crossbar or glove, designed to fit over the knuckles or concealed against the palm and slash through skin and muscle.


Cestus
Ancient battle glove used in pankration and gladiatorial bouts, made of leather strips, sometimes filled with iron plates or fitted with studs.
Myrmex ("limb-piercer") - Roman variation fitted with spikes.
Sphairai - Greek variation fitted with cutting blades.


Emeici ("emei piercers")
Traditional Chinese martial arts weapon consisting of a pair of sharp-ended metal rods mounted on a detachable ring worn on the middle finger, allowing them to be spun to distract an opponent and delicately manipulated.


Knuckledusters (brass knuckles, knucks)
Pieces of metal shaped to fit around the knuckles, designed to preserve and focus a punch's force by directing it toward a smaller and harder contact area, resulting in increased tissue disruption along with an increased chance of fracturing bone.


Madu (maru, singuata)
Indian parrying and thrusting weapon consisting of a pair of antelope horns fastened with the tips pointing in opposite directions behind a small plate of stretched leather, iron, or steel to protect the hand.


War Fan
Fan designed for use in warfare, often employed by the samurai class in feudal Japan. The Japanese art of fighting with fans is called tessenjutsu, though as often the fans were used simply as signaling devices.
Tessen - folding fans with outer spokes made of heavy iron plates designed to look like harmless folding fans or solid clubs shaped to resemble a closed fan. Used for fending off darts and arrows, as a throwing weapon, and as an aid for swimming.
Gunpai (dansen uchiwa) - large, solid open fans carried by high-ranking samurai officers that are constructed of solid iron, metal with a wooden core, or solid wood. Used to ward off arrows, provide shade, and signal troops.
*Korean War Fan - Made of "pak dahl" wood, an exceptionally resilient birch tree that thrived in harsh seasons and ice storns, tempered to a hardness that could repel contemporary edged weapons. Developed in the Joseon Dynasty, they were popular among the middle (chungin) and upper class (yangban), and are still used in Korean martial arts today. Korean war fans are often unique to their weilder, some weaving metal ribbons along the outermost edge for cutting power, others using feathers to hide finger-sized razor blades. Warriors of the Kook Sool Won (Guksulwon) school practice a technique / throwing blades were concealed and released in a spread by snapping the fan open.
*Poison Fans - deadly or stunning concoctions were hid in channels or bladders which would open upon spreading the fan, allowing the user to direct a gust of toxins or irritants at their foes over a short distance.

KNIVES


Athame
An orrnate black-handled and double-edged ritual knife used in Ceremonial magick and Wiccan traditions.


Balisong(butterfly knife)
A small hinged folding knife constructed so that the blade is hidden within the handles when closed.


Ballistic Knife
Knife with a spring-loaded detachable blade that ejects with the push of a trigger to an effective range of 5 m (16ft) at a speed of 39 mph / 63 Km/h. Variations using compressed air, gas propulsion, or even an explosive charge like a blank pistol cartridge, are somewhat more powerful and do not suffer from spring fatigue. Ballistic knives briefly gained noteriety in the 1980s when they were marketed commercially for a brief time and (false) rumours spread that they were originally deveoped by the Soviet Special Forces for the spetsnaz units.


Baselard
14th c. large dagger with an H-shaped handle, evolving out of the 13th c. knightly dagger.


Bayonet
Any knife-like close-quarters weapon designed to attach to the muzzle of a gun.


Bollock Dagger (ballock knife, kidney knife)
Dagger with a distinctly-shaped shaft featuring two oval swellings at the guard resembling male genetalia (hence "bollocks"). The guard is often in piece with the wooden grip. The dagger was used in a manner similar to the Scottish dirk and was popular in England, Flanders, Scandinavia, and Scotland from the 13th to 18th c.
*Dudgeon Dagger - 16 -17th c variant with a hilt constructed of box root (dudgeon).


Bowie Knife
Fixed-blade fighting knife popularized by and named for Colonel Jim Bowie, characterized as a large sheath knife with a cross guard and a clip point. The blade is typically 9.5" (24 cm) long, 1/4" (.65 cm) thick, and1.5" (3.8 cm) wide.


Cinquedea ("five-fingers")
15th - 16th c. Italy civilian dagger whose name refers to the width of the blade at the base. Used primarily as a thrusting weapon, the heavy blade is about 18" (45cm) long and tapered to a somewhat rounded point, while the grip features a small pommel and a guard curved with the concave side towards the point.


Dirk
A long thrusting dagger first appearing in 17th c, Scotland as the personal sidearm of officers in Scottish Highland regiments. During the Age of Sail it was popular as the personal weapon of officers involved in hand-to-hand naval combat. Often used as a generic term for dagger.

Ear Dagger
Exotic dagger used during the late Middle Ages and Renaissance, named for its distinctive-shaped pommel and featuring a single sharpened edge ending in a point.


Hachiwara (Jap., "helmet breaker" or "skull breaker")
Weapon carried as a side-arm by samurai, usually around 13.65" (350 mm) long, resembling a jutte in many respects. The blade is a curved tapered square iron or steel bar with a hook on its back edge, forged with a sharp, dirk-like point. Designed for parrying, hooking the cords of armour or helmets, and separating armour plates like a can opener.


Kris (kalis, keris)
Assymetrical dagger with a distinctive wavy blade, similar to that of the flamberge sword, strongly associated with the culture of Indonesia (though also indigenous to Malaysia and Thailand), where it is regarded as both a weapon and spiritual object. Kris are often considered to possess an essence or presence, magical powers, and some blades are thought inherently lucky or unlucky.


Machete
Large cleaver-like cutting tool with a blade typically 13 to 18" (32.5 - 45 cm) long and under .15" (3 mm) thick, frequently used in various tropical and subtropical countries for the cutting of rainforest undergrowth, vines, and sugar canes, as well as the popular weapon of choice for civil uprisings.


Misericorde ("mercy-giver")
12th c. long, narrow knife with a blade thin enough to strike through the gaps in armour plates or helmet visors. Used to deliver the death stroke to a wounded knight.


Parrying Dagger (main-gauche Fr. 'left hand")
A category of small handheld daggers used as offhand weapons in conjunction with a single-handed sword. Designed for defense, they sport a wider guard and often other features to protect the hand.
*Sword-Breaker - heavy dagger with slots forming comb-like "teeth" on one side, allowing the user to catch an opponent's blade and hold it fast. It is dubious that it was capable of actually breaking swords.
*Trident Dagger - long, thin dagger with twin blades that spring outward from either side of the main blade, used to trap opponent'sweapons.


Poignard/Poniard
A long, lightweight thrusting knife similar to a parrying dagger, with a crossguard and tapering, acutely pointed blade. Worn by the upper class of Western Europe during the Middle Ages and Renaissance.


Push Dagger (fist knife, gimlet knife, push dirk, punch dagger, stoßdolch, faustmesser)
A short-bladed dagger with a "T" handle designed to be grasped in the hand so that the blade protrudes from the front of the 2nd and 3rd fingers of the fist. During the later half of the 19th c. it enjoyed a brief period of popularity in Britain and central Europe, especially Germany, where its thought they were introduced in the mid-1800s by foreign sailors. Subsequently, German cutlery makers manufactured domestic versions marketed as a self-defence weapon for travellers, often set in nickel-silver mountings.


Katar (suwaiya) - Type of push dagger from India characterized by its "H"- shaped horizontal hand grip, resulting in the blade sitting above the user's knuckles. Ceremonial variations were used in worship.

Rondel Dagger ("round/circular dagger")
14th c. dagger used by European merchants and nobles with a 12" (30 cm) long, slim steel blade, sharpened on one or both edges and tapering to a needle point. It takes its name from its round or octagonal handguard and pommel. The blade is lenticular, triangular or diamond-shaped at the crross section and were primarily designed for use with a stabbing action or cutting, ideal for puncturing chain mail. Though not capable of puncturing plate armour, a Rondel can be forced between the joints of a suit of armour or gaps in the helm. By the 15th c. it was the standard sidearm for knights,
*Four-Edged Rondel Daggers - variation with a cruciform blade, unsuitable for use as a general cutting tool rather exclusively used for thrusting attacks in battle, foreshadowing the stiletto.


Sai
Traditional Eastern martial arts weapon taking the form of a pointed, prong-shaped metal baton with two curved prongs (yoku) projecting from the handle, typically used in pairs, It is primarily used as a striking weapon for short jabs into an opponent's solar plexus and for parrying, though there are numerous ways of wielding the versatile weapon, allowing it to inflict lethal or non-lethal attacks.
Chabang (tjabang) - Indonesian form of the sai used in the fighting art pentjak-silat.


Schweizerdolch (Holbien dagger, Swiss dagger)
Distinctive 16th c dagger featuring two crescent-shaped, inward-bent metal bars delimiting the curved hilt with a double-edged blade tapering to a point.


Seax (sax, sæx, sex, Lat. sachsum)
Dagger or short sword typical of migration period and early Middle Ages Germanic tribes. The Saxons derived their name from this weapon, and in O.E. it became a general term for "knife". The Seax is characterized by a large single-edged blade with the tang in the centerline inserted into a wood or horn hilt, worn horizontally in a scabbard attached to the belt, the edge of the blade upwards. There are many variatons in size occuring from 450 to 800AD, with the Long Seaxes, appearing at the end of the 7th c. possessing blades of 50cm or more.


Sgian-Dubh
A small, single-edged knife (Gael. sgian) worn as part of the official Scottish Highland dress. Though the primary meaning of "dubh" is 'black', the knife takes iits name from the secondary meaning of 'hidden', as in the Gaelic dubh-fhacal "riddle" (lit. "hidden word")or dubh-cheist "enigma" (lit. "hidden question"). The origin of the term lies in the custom of hospitality wherein guests were required to disarm at the door before entering a house. The sgian-dubh was retained, concealed in the hose or boot. A short blade of 3 to 3.5 " (76 - 89 mm) with a scalloped or 'spear-point' tip is typical, with a hilt of horn or,more commonly, antler, the sgian-dubh resembles small skinning knives forund in most basic sets of hunting knives.
*Sgian-Achlais - a small twin-edged dagger concealed under the armpit


Stiletto
A knife or dagger with a long slender blade ending in a needle-like point, intended as a deep-penetrating stabbing weapon. First appeared in Italy in the 14th c., probably as a development of the misericordia or rondel dagger, and quickly became popular among knights for dealing the mercy-strike or 'coup-de-gras'. It was later adopted across Italy as the favoured offensive thrusting knife (arma manesca) of the medieval assassin, as it was easily concealable and effective at penetrating the heavy leather and fabric clothing of the day while inflicting mortal wounds that tended to bleed less or slower. This association eventually led to the stiletto becoming prohibited by law as a 'treacherous weapon' (arma insidiosa). A combatant skilled in the use of the stiletto thrusts the knife deep into the victim, rapidly twisting the blade sharply in several directions before extracting it, causing severe internal damage not readily apparent from the entrance wound. Variations were crafted with narrow grooves running the length of the blade, which served as channels to funnel poison into the victim. The Stiletto remained popular well into the twentieth century and was frequently carried by soldiers in the first and second World Wars. Its popularity in Sicily led to the establishment of the scherma di stiletto siciliano ("Sicilian scholl of stiletto fighting").


Switchblade (automatic knife, ejector knife, flick knife, pushbutton knife, Sprenger)
Small knife with a folding or sliding blade contained in the handle which is opened by a spring when a button or switch is activated, originating in mid-18th c Europe.


Trench Knife
A category of knives used in trench warfare of the 1st and 2nd World War, characterized by an integral D-shaped hand guard.

FLAILS
Tool for threshing crops consisting of a wooden staff with a short heavy stick attached to the top, usually by a flexible link of chain or rope. Weaponized flails are variations on this theme.


Furi-Zue (brandishing sticks)
Class of Japanese weapons including any type of staff or stick with a chain attached.
*Chigiriki - Japanese flail weapon consisting of a solid or hollow wood (sometimes bamboo) or iron staff with an iron weight or ball and chain on the end, occasionally retractable.The iron weight might have spikes, while its shape may be round or multi-sided.

*Kasurigama (Jap., "chain-sickle") - weapon consisting of a kama (sickle) on a metal chain, with a heavy iron weight at the end. The art of handling the kasurigama is called kusarigamajutsu.
Kusari-Fundo (Jap., manrikigusari, "ten thousand power chain")
Hand weapon consisting of a length of chain (kusari) from 12 to 48", with a weight (fundo) attached to either end of the chain. The shape of the weights is incredibly variable.


Kyoketsu-Shoge (Jap., "to run about in the fields and mountains")
Weapon used almost exclusivelly by the ninja consisting of a double-edged blade, with another curved blade near the hilt at a 45-60 degree angle, attached to a 12 to 18' length of rope or chain which ends in a metal ring.


Military Flail (French fléau d'armes, German Kriegsflegel)
Consists of a wooden shaft joined by a length of chain to one or more iron-shod wooden bars or heavy sword pommels.
*Holy Water Sprinkler (French goupillon)- variation named for its resemblance to the aspergillum used in Catholic mass, featuring an all-steel head with six flanges forming 3 spikes each, reminiscent of a macehead but with a short thick spike of square coss section extending from the top.
*Kettenmorgenstern (German, lit. "chain-morningstar") - flail equipped with a single spiked metal ball attached by a chain to a wooden shaft.
**Rentsuru - Japanese chain morning star, called "meteor hammer" in China.
*Pyeongon - Korean military flail.


Nunchucks (Jap., nunchaku)
Traditional Okinawan weapon consisting of 2 sticks connected at one end with a short chain (kusari) or cord (himo). In China, the striking stick is called "dragon stick" while the handle is called "yang stick". Chinese nunchucks are rounded, whereas the Okinawan version has an octogonal cross-section (allowing one edge of the nunchuka to make contact on the target for increased damage). The optimal length of each piece is long enough to protect the forearm when held in a high grip near the top of the shaft, while the optimallength of cord is just enough for the user to lay it over his or her palm, with the sticks hanging comfortably and perpendicular to the ground. Traditionally nunchaku are carved from a strong, flexible hardwood such as loquat, oak, or pasania, submerged in mud for several years causing it to harden, connected with a cord of horsehair.
*Bleeder - nunchaku with razor blades. Part of training in French nunchaku de combat.
*Sharper - nunchaku with nails. Part of training in French nunchaku de combat.
*Telescopic Nunchaku - nunchaku with retractable metal sticks.


Sansetsukonn (panlong gub, sānjiégùn)
Also called "coiling dragon staff", Chinese flail weapon consisting of three wooden or metal staffs connected by metal rings or a rope. A more complex version of the nunchaku or two-section staff, the staves can be spun to gather momentum and act as an extension of the user's arm to strike, block, choke, trap, or disarm, with different sections of the staff acting at the same time. The chains or ropes of the staff are used to entangle an opponent and their weapon. It can be used at long-range (whip), intermediate range(flail), or close range (escrima), most advantageous as a short range weapon against long range weapons. In this configuration, a skilled user can nearly simultaneously block an opponent's strike, and trap their weapon while executing their own strike with the free side of the staff. Historically carved from red maple, waxwood, or white oak, modern staves areconstructed from bamboo, rattan, or aluminum. The ideal length of the three staffs is as long as the wielder's arm with a combined diameter that fits easily in the hand.

WHIPS

It was rare for combatants to use whips in battle, as they were primarily designed to inflict pain. A skilled wielder can use a whip as an extension of their arm or hand, and the whip is cracking good for making a loud noice that causes a fear response (indeed, a whip was the first man-made object to break the sound barrier). However, while capable of dealing out nasty welts and fearsome stings, whips typically aren't capable of inflicting debilitating injury. If the whip sports sharp barbs or the tip is constructed of a material strong and thin enough to cut, like Kevlar, a whip may inflict more serious wounds, but still a disabling injury is unlikely. A whip can wrap around an opponent's limbs or neck, but this is generally impracticle in battle where maintaining the rather precise necessary distance to make the whip effective is quite improbable. Nevertheless, there are a few weaponized whips, often incorporating elements or aspects of flails.

*Jiujiebian (Chinese, "9 section whip") - segmented metal chain whip designed for use in martial arts.
*Sjambok (litupa) - Heavy leather whip of South Africa, traditionally made of rhinoceros or hippo hide, cut into a strip 3 to 5' (0.9 - 1.5 cm) long, tapering from 1" (25mm) thick at the handle to 3/8" (10mm) at the tip, and rolled between heavy plates until reaching a tapered-cylindrical form.
*Stockwhips - A type of single-tailed leather whip with a short handle and exceptionally long lash. Utilized primarily for driving/directing animals with its crack, its not typically used to actually strike an animal, as it is difficult to apply with precuision and would inflict severe pain.
**Bullwhip - stockwhip composed of a braided leather thong, with a handle 8 to 12" in length and a lash between 3 and 20'.

POLEARMS


Glaive
European polearm consisting of a of a 6 or 7' (2 m) long pole terminating in a single-edged 18" (45 cm) blade, affixed in a socket-shaft configuration similar to an axehead.

*Glaive-guisarme - glaive with a small hook on the reverse side of the blade.

*Bardiche (berdiche, "long poll axe") - Eastern European glaive-like polearm originating in 15th c. Russia, descendant of the medieval sparth (Dane axe). The blade was often very long for an axe, exceeding 2', while thje shaft was the shortest of any pole-arms, rarely extending beyond 5' (1.5m), relying more on the weight of the blace than the momentum of a swing.

*Fauchard - Type of pole-arm used in medieval Europe from the 11th to 14th c., consisting of a curved blade 6 to 7" (15 - 18 cm) attached to a 6' to 7' (1.8 - 2.1 m) long pole. The blade bears a moderate curve, however unlike the glaive the cutting edge is on the concave side, as with a scythe or sickle. Later variants have one or more spear points attached to the back or top of the blade, sometimes confused with the guisarme.

*Guan Dao (kwan dao, kuan tao, Chi., yănyuèdāo, "reclining moon blade") - Chinese pole-arm comparable to the fauchard or glaive, consisting of a heavy blade with a spike at the back and a notch at the spike's upper base that can catch an opponent's weapon as well as irregular serrations leading the back edge of the blade to the spike, mounted atop a 5 - 6' (1.5 - 1.8 m) long wooden or metal pole.

* Naginata - Variation of the Japanese nihontō in the form of a pole-arm, originally used by the samurai as well foot soldiers (ashigaru) & warrior monks (sōhei). A naginata consists of a 1.2 - 2.5 m wooden shaft with a 30 - 60 cm long curved blade at the end, similar to the glaive.

*Svärdstav (Swedish, lit. "sword-staff") - Medieval polearm consisting of a 2-edged sword blade attached to a 6.5' (2 m) staff, often equipped with sword-like quilons.

*Voulge (pole cleaver) - A curved blade attached to a pole by binding the lower 2/3 of the blade to the side of the pole, similar to the glaive and Sparth axe.

Lance
Long spear designed specifically to be used by a mounted warrior as a thrusting weapon, equipped with a vamplate to prevent the wielder's hand from sliding up the shaft on impact.
*Kontos - (Greek "oar" or "barge-pole") - long wooden cavalry lance developed in the early-mid 1st c AD from shorter spearlike weapons, used extensively by Iranian cavalry. It was weilded with two hands, with the horses directed by the rider's knees; this made it a specialist weapon that required extrensive training and exceptional horsemanship to use. Romans adopted a variation transliterated as contus. The name is the stem of multiple derivative cavalry lances of the region like the Hungarian gönder, the Turkish rumh ("Roman lance"), and Arabic quntariya.
**Kontarian - later Byzantine variation wielded by the cataphracts from c. 1100, useed single-handed couched under the armpit as with the contemporary knightly lance.
*Spontoon (espontoon, "half-pike") - European lance that developed alongside the pike, in wide use by the 17th c with continued use up to the mid to late 19th c. Typically 6 or 7' in overall length, with a head distinguished from the pike by a pair of smaller blades on either side.


Military Fork
Pole-arm used in Europe between the 15th and 19th c., consisting of a pitchfork-like head with only two prongs, longer and straighter in comparison to a pitchfork or trident. The pair of tines usually ran parallel or slightlfy flared.
*Corseque (chave-souris) - 16th to 17th c. European pole-arm with a three-bladed head, similar to a winged spera or spetum, on a 6 - 8' (1.8 - 2.5 m) haft. There are two main variants, one with the side blades ('flukes' or 'wings') branching from the neck of the central blade at 45 degrees, the other with hooked blades curving back towards the haft.
*Ranseur (runkah, rawcon) - 15th c European polearm, 6' long with a head consisting of a spear-tip affized with a crescent-shaped cross hilt at its base, resembling a sai mounted on a staff.
*Spetum (chave souris, corseca, corseque) - 13th c European polearm, 6 - 8' long featuring a main blade of 12 - 14" & 2 prongs half that length set at acute angles at the base of the spearhead. Distinguished from the Ranseur by its prongs being single-edged and used for slashing.
*Trident (gig, leister, trishula) - Three-pronged spear used for fishing. It gained favour as a military weapon for its long reach and ability to trap other long weapons to disarm the weilder. Famously used together with a net by a type of Roman gladiator called a retiarius ("net fighter").


Pike
Anti-cavalry thrusting spear used extensively by infantry from the early Middle Ages until the 17th c. It varied considerably in size, from 10 to 25' (3 - 7.5 m) in length, weighing approx. 17 to 22 lbs (8 - 10 kg). The shaft near the head is often reinforced with metal strips ("cheeks" or langets).
*Ahlspiess (Awl pike) - Thrusting spear developed and popular in 15 -16th c. Germany and Austria, consisting a long thin spike of square cross section 3.5' (1 m) or more in length, mounted to a round wooden shaft 5 to 6' (1.6 - 1.8 m) long and secured with a pair of langets extending from the socket. At the base of the spike was a rondel guard (a circular metal plate) to protect the hands.
**Breach Pikes - variation of ahlspiess lacking a rondel guard.
**Candeliere - Italian variation of ahlspiess with thicker, rounder, & shorter spikes, named for a round candlestick of the period in the centre of which was a small pricket to hold the candle in place.
*Atgeir (hewing spear, mail-piercer) - Polearm used in Viking age Scandinavia and Iceland and the British Isles, closely resembling a bill or glaive. Later became a general term for European halberds.
**Atgeirsstafir - later multi-purpose staves with spearheads.
*Boar Spear - Short, heavy spear with two "lugs" or "wings" on the spearsocket behind the blade.
**Chiavarina - longer style boar spear developed in 15th c. for warfare.
*Brandistock - Short (5') spear with a retractable blade/spike.
**Buttafuore ("feather staff") - variation with 3 retractable spikes.
*Dory (doru) - Chief armament of the hoplites. 3m (10') long with a flat leaf-shaped iron spearhead counter-balanced by a bronze butt-spike. Used single-handed.
*Hasta, pl. Hastae - Roman spear 2m (6.5') with an ash-wood shaft and iron spearhead.
**Hasta Pura - ceremonial spear made without iron, awarded as a military decoration.
*Menavlion (menaulion, manavlon) - Heavy thrusting spear with a length of 9 to 12' (2.7 - 3.6 m), used by Byzantine infantry as early as the 10th c. AD against heavy cavalry. To give it increased strength, whole oak or cornal saplings were used, tipped with a long blade of 18 - 20' (45 - 50 cm).
*Sarissa/Sarisa - 4 - 7m (13-21') Hellenic spear made of tough & resilent cornel wood, thus quite heavy (13 - 15 lbs / 5.5 - 6.6 kg). It had a sharp iron leaf-shaped head counter-balanced by a bronze butt-spike used to anchor it to the ground to stop charges.


Pollax (pole-axe, polax)
In the 14th c. the basic long axe gained an armour-piercing spike one the back and another on the end of the haft for thrusting, deventually developing into the pollaxe in the 15th c. The polaxe emerged in response to the need for weapons that could penetrate plate armour and variations feature a myriad of combinations of axe-blade, back spike, and hammer.
*Bec de Corbin (Old Fr, "crow's beak") - Popular medieval polearm consisting of a modified hammer's head and spike mounted atop a long pole, similar to the Lucerne hammer. Unlike the Lucerne hammer, the bec de corbin was used to attack primarily with the stouter fluke or 'beak', better designed for tearing armour, rather than the hammer head, which was blunt as opposed to the multi-pronged Lucerne. Additionally, the spike atop the bec de corbin's head wasn't nearly as long or thin.
*Bec de Faucon (Old Fr, "falcon's beak") - poleaxe with a pronounced hook on its reversed side.
*Bill (bill-guisarme, bill hook, English bill) - Medieval European polearm, similar in size, function, and appearance to the halberd but with the blade tapering to a pronounced hook.
**Guisarme (bisarme, gisarme, giserne) - Pole-arm popular in Europe from 1000 to 1400, used primarily to dismount knights and horsemen. While early designs were simply a hook on the end of a long pole, later versions implemented a small reverse spike on the back of the blade.
*Doloire (wagoner's axe) - tool and weapon used during the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, derived from the Latin dolabra, a tool axe used by Roman legionaries. It has a wooden shaft approx. 5' (1.5 m) long and a head pointed at the top and rounded at the bottom, resembling a tear drop or isosceles triangle. The axe head itself is approx. 17" (44 cm), sharpened on the back and flattened bottom edges, and uniformly decorated with abstract floral patterns The upper part of the blade extended above the eye, with the opposite side of the socket featuring a small blunt hammer head.
**Spiez Doloire (Swiss doloire) - a doloire with an opposing fluke or spike in place of a hammer.
*Halberd (halbard, halbert, Swiss voulge) - two-handed polearm that came to prominance in the 14 and 15th c., consisting of an axe blade topped with a long thin spike and featuring a thorn or hook on the reverse side, mounted on a 5 to 6' (1.5 - 1.8 m) long shaft, reinforced with metal rims.
*Lucerne Hammer - 15th to 17th c. Swiss polearm combining the war hammer with the bec de corbin, with the 'hammer' actually a 3 or 4-pronged head mounted atop a 7' (2 m) shaft.
*Ono (masa-kari) - Japanese term for "axe" or "hatchet", those designed for military use were rare, with a large head and convex cutting edge and a scroll-shaped peen opposite, of length up to 6'.
*Sparth Axe - 13th c. pollaxe variant of the Dane Axe, with a heavy crescent-shaped head mounted on a shaft 4 to 6' (1.2 - 1.8 m) long. Adopted by the Anglo-Saxons and Normans in the 11th c. with variants used in Scotland and Ireland until the 16th c.
**Spontoon-Axe - variation of the spontoon with a shorter haft and the blades mounted on the side of the weapon.


Scythe (O.E. siðe, sithe)
Farming implement frequently used as a makeshift polearm, consisting of a S-curved 67" (170 cm) wooden shaft (snathe, snathe, or snaith), with 2 short handles set at right angles; one roughly in the middle and one closer to the top; and a long, curved blade of 24 to 35" (60 - 90 cm) mounted perpendicular to the snaith at the lower end. The blade edge is peened almost as thin as paper. Using the scythe is a skilled task, performed with ease by experienced reapers but clumsily and with great effort by beginners.

*Dagger-Axe (Chinese, gē, ko, pinyin) - Weapon consisting of a dagger-like blade made of jade, bronze, or, later, iron, mounted by the tang to a perpendicular wooden shaft with a spear point.

STAVES


Bō (Korean bong, Chinese kon)
A class of long staff weapons uased in Okinawa and feudal Japan. The most common versions, sometimes specified as rokushakubō, were 6' (1.8m) long and 1.25" (3 cm) thick, gradually tapering from the middle to .75" (2 cm) at the end. Most were round (maru-bo), but square (kaku-bo), hexagonal (rokkaku-bo), and octogonal (hakkaku-bo) variations were known. The kyu-shaku-bō was the longest variation, at 9' (2.7m).The Japanese martial art of weilding the bō is bōjutsu, based on te ("hand") empty-hand thrusting, swinging, and striking techniques derived from Quanfa of the Chinese monks.


Eku (eiku, ieku, Ryuku oar)
Ancient weapon of Okinawan kobudō originating from an oar, 160 cm in length, traditionally adapted for use as a weapon by fishermen. The eiku is heavier than the bō, with a displaced centre of gravity and thus considered more difficult to master.


Jō
Japanese staff weapon shorter than the bō, approx. 4.2' (1.3 m) in length. The martial art of using the jō is jōjutsu or jōdō. Used primarily against swords while the bō staff was used against spears.


Jutte (jitte, Japanese, lit. "ten hand", i.e. 'weapon with the power of 10 hands')
Specialist stick weapon roughly 18" (45 cm) with a one-pronged tine, around 5 cm long, starting just above the handle and pointing to the tip. Though it could be used to parry blades, it is primarily employed to hook into clothing or parts of the body like mouth or nostrils. During the Edo period, the jutte was carried as a badge of office, especially by samuari law enforcement officers called okappiki or doshin. The jutte is the subject of the martial art of juttejutsu.


Kurunthadi (cheruvadi, kuruvadi, muchan)
Indian stick weapon with a length of 2.5' (76 cm), made from the wood of the tamarind tree. Trained in by students of Kalarippayattu, used to practice the basics of dagger-fighting.


Otta
Indian wooden stick with an S-shaped curve and rounded tip. Considered a 'master weapon', the most difficult weapon students of Kalarippayattu are trained in. Used by skilled wielders to strike the vital points (marma) of an opponent's body.


Quarterstaff
Traditional European pole weapon, a shaft of hardwood 6 to 9' (2 - 2.75 m) long, sometimes tipped with iron. Variatons had a ferrule or spike at both ends. Sometimes referred to as a short staff in comparison to the pike as a "long staff" in excess of 11 or 12' (3.5 - 3.75 m).
*Naboot (asa, asaya, nabboot, shoum) - quarterstaff constructed of palm wood or rattan, originating in Egypt and used in the martial art Tahtib.


Sally Rod
Long, thin willow stick, used as a disciplinary instrument and sometimes as a weapon in Ireland.

The Right Hand of Doom

124,996
  • Apr 27, 2017
  • #22

Shareeravadi
Bamboo staff with a length extending from the weilder's neck to their feet. Second of three weapons trained in by students of Kalarippayattu, used to practice the basics of spear-fighting.


Shinobi Zue
Ninja staffs or canes, often hollow with multiple uses such as hiding other weapons (blowguns, shuriken, etc) or a breathing tube.


Taiaha
Traditional weapon of the New Zealand Māori, a staff of wood or whale bone between 5 to 6' (1.5 - 1.8 m) long with 3 main parts: the arero (tongue) for stabbing and parrying, the upoko (head, the base from which the tongue protrudes), and the ate (liver) or tinana (body), also used for striking and parrying. Its use is taught along with other Māori weapons in the martial art Mau rakau. A ceremonial Taiaha is commonly given to visiting dignitaries.


Tambo
Short staff weapon used in Okinawa and feudal Japan, less than 3.3' (1 m) long. Swung using the elbow and shoulder, or manipulated by the wrist, many of the motions similar to sword strokes. Its main purpose is to attack the outer edges of human bones with speed and accuracy and they can be quite deadly in skilled hands. Variations over 3.3' (1 m) long are called Hanbo.


Tewhatewha
Long-handled Māori club weapon, shaped like an axe and commonly decorated with pigeon or hawk feathers. Unlike an axe, the blows are not struck with the blade, rather the thicker straight front edge.


Tonfa (tong fa, tuifa, Chinese, pinyin, Okinawan, tonfaa)
Stick weapons 15 - 20" (38 - 50 cm) long with wooden handles, almost always used in pairs.

SWORDS

BACKSWORDS
Swords characterized by a straight single-edged blade and a hilt with a single-handed grip. It takes its name from the triangular cross-section which gives a flat back edge opposite the cutting edge. From the early 14th c, the backsword became the first type of European sword to be fitted with a kncuckle-guard. Often the secondary weapon of cavalrymen in the late 16th and early 17th c.


Claidheamh Cuil (Scot. Gaelic, "back sword")
Backsword used by the Highland Scots.


Dāo (dan dao) - the Chinese sabre, with a moderately-curved single-edged blade with a canted hilt, curving in the opposite direction of the blade, and a disc or cup-shaped guard. Cord is usually wrapped over the the wood of the handle, and the hilt is often pierced for the addition of lanyards or tassels. In Chinese folklore it is known as "The General of Weapons", considered one of the 4 major weapons of China, along with the sword (Jian), staff (Gun), and spear (Qiang).
*Changdāo (miao dāo, Chinese, lit. "long sabre") - anti-cavalry sword used during the Ming Dynasty with a length of 1.95 m with a long handle, slightly more than 1/3 of the weapon's total length, and a slightly more curved blade than the Japanese ōdachi. It replaced the earlier zhăn mă dāo.
*Dàdāo (Chinese, "great sabre") - Variation of the Chinese sabre with a broad blade between 2 to 3' long, a long hilt meant for two-handed use, and a weight-forward balance. Though some were made for military use, its more commonly associated with civilian militias and revolutionaries. While not a particularly sophisticated sword, its weight and blance gives it chopping power, making it an effective close combat weapon for untrained combatants. Also frequently used by executioners for beheadings. Its origin, design, and use is broadly comparable to the European falchion.
*Liuyedāo (Chinese, "willow-leaf sabre") - type of dāo commonly used as a military sidearm for both cavalry & infantry during the Ming & Qing dynasties, featuring a moderate curve along the 36 to 39", slightly reducing thrusting ability while increasing the power of slashes and cuts. It weighs 2 - 3 lbs.
*Miao Dāo (Chinese, "sprout sabre") - two-handed sabre with a narrow blade up to 1.2 m or more in length and a long hilt, the name referring to the likeness of the weapon to a newly sprouted plant.
*Piandāo - deeply curved dāo meant for slashing and draw-cutting, bearing a strong resemblance to a scimitar. A fairly uncommon weapon, it was generally used in conjunction with a shield.
*Wodāo ("blade of the wo people") - Chinese sword of the Ming dynasty featuring a slender, gently-curving blade 31" (80 cm) long, with a 10" (25.5 cm) handle. Also used by Japanese samurai.
*Yanmaodāo ("goose-quill sabre") - type of dāo used as a standard military weapon during the Ming and Qing dynasties. The blade is sttraight until the last 1/4th or so approaching the tip, referred to as the centre of percussion. The centre of percussion is the point on the blade with the least vibration on hard contact, i.e. the spot on the blade that transmits the most power to a target on a hard chop. Its grip is straight, in contrast with the more common curving grips.
*Zhăn Mă Dāo ("horse-chopping sabre") - single-bladed anti-cavalry sword resembling a nagamaki in construction, with a wrapped handle 15" (37 cm) long, intended to be gripped two-handed. The blade is 3.75' (115 cm)long and very straight up until a slight curve at the very end.


Falchion 11th c. European one-handed, single-edged backsword whose design is reminiscent of the Persian Scimitar, originating from the seax. It combines the versatility of a sword with the power and weight of an axe, including a single edge with a slight curve towards the point of the blade with a quilloned crossguard for the hilt in the manner of an arming sword. There are two basic types:
*Cleaver Falchion - 13th to 14th c., shaped much like a large meat cleaver or machete.
*Cusped Falchion - 38" (95.5 cm) long, with a 31.5" (80 cm) straight blade bearing a cusped or flare-clipped tip similar to the later Turkish kilij. A 13th c. variation is mounted on a 1 to 2' (30 - 61 cm) long wooden haft, sometimes endin in a curve like an umbrella.


Sabre
Type of backsword with a curved, single-edged slashing blade & large handguard, covering the knuckles of the hand as well as the thumb & forefinger. Most are carried in a scabbard hanging from a shoulder belt (baldric) or from a waist-mounted sword belt, usually with slings of different lengths to permit the scabbard to hand below a rider's waist level. Sabre-like curved backswords have been in use in Europe since the medieval period (falchion, paramērion), but the introduction of the sabre proper to Western Europe dates to the 17th c, inspired by the Eastern European szabla. Sabrage is the act of opening a champagne bottle with a sabre.
*Briquet - Infantry sabre of the Napoleonic Wars.
*Cutlass - short, broad sabre with a straight or slightly-curved blade sharpened on the cutting edge, and a hilt featuring a solid cupped guard. It was a common naval weapon.
*Boarding Sabre - short sabre used by 19th c. naval officers.
*Paramērion - Curved, slashing sword 94 cm in length, used by Byzantine cavalry.
*Shashka - a very sharp, single-edged and handed sabre effective for both thrusting and slashing. It features a hollowed or fullered blade with no guard, but a large, curved pommel.


Scimitar (Arabic, saif, sief, mohannad; Fr., cimeterre; Italy, scimitarra; Afghanistan, pulwar; Algeria, flyssa; Egypt, kilij; Ethiopia, shotel; India, talwar; Iran, shamshir; Morocco,nimcha; Sudan, kaskara; Tuareg, takoba) - a backsword or sabre with a distinctly curved blade that broadens towards the point, originating in Persia and widespread throughout the muslim world.
*Claidheam Crom (Scot Gaelic, "crooked sword") - Celtic sword with a scimitar-style blade ("Turcael"), such as that wielded by Alasdair Mor, the Champion of Clan Grant.
*Mameluke Sword - 18th - 19th c. Egyptian scimitar adopted by the British and French.


Szabla
Eastern European one-edged sabre-like sword with a curved blade ending in a two-bladed tip called a feather (pióro).
*Czeczuga - szabla featuring a small cross-guard with an ornamented open hilt and a hood offering partial protection to the hand.
*Karabela - (Turk. kara-bela, "black-bane") szabla modelled after the swords of the Turkish Huzzars but lighter, with an open hilt, the pommel modeled after an eagle's head. This grip allowed for easier handling of ciurcular cuts while fighting on foot, and swinging cuts from horseback.
*Ordynkya - szabla used by cavalry, resembling the czeczuga with a heavier hilt & shorter blade.

BROADSWORDS
Largest category of swords, termed "broad" in contrast to the rapier & other 'light bladed' swords, referring as much to length as width. The term was used synonymously with "hand-and-a-half" sword and "longsword", though here I've made a distinction between thinner-bladed longswords of the rapier-type (Longswords) and wider-bladed longswords of the basket-hilted-type (Broadswords). The prototypical broadsword arose during the late medieval & Renaissance periods (~1350 to1550), called espadón, montante, or mandoble in Spain and sadone or spada longa in Italy, characterized by a cruciform hilt for two-handed use, a straight double-edged blade of around 39 to 48" (100 - 122 cm) in length, and a weight of 2.5 to 3.3 lbs (1.1 - 1.5 kg).

Basket-Hilted Sword
Group of early modern broadswords characterized by a basket-shaped guard, a development of the quillons added to swords' crossguards since the late Middle Ages.

*Claidheamh Caol (Scot Gaelic, "narrow sword") - basket-hilted sword whose blade resembles a shorter version of the claymore, which it is often mistaken for by modern authors. It is called 'narrow' in comparison to the claymore (broadsword), but is roughly the size of contemporary longswords.

*Mortuary Sword - After the execustion of King Charles I (1649), basket-hilted swords were made which depicted the face or "death mask" of the martyred king. These came to be known as "mortuary swords", and were primarily used by Scot cavalry.

*Shiavona - Renaissance sword popular in the 16th and 17th c., widely recognisable for its "cat's-head pommel" and distinctive handguard made up of many leaf-shaped brass or iron bars attached to the cross-bar and knucklebow rather than the pommel. The blade is double-edged and measures around 1 yd (93 cm) in length, bearing two fullers or grooves running about 1/4 the length of the *Sinclair Hilt - one of the earliest basket-hilt designs, German in origin. The blade is 38" (96.5 cm), with a triangular plate similar to that of main gauche daggers, decorated with pierced hearts and diamonds. Used in the mid-18th c. by the Austrian army and inspired by the British heavy cavalry sabre, its believed these swords were brought back to Scotland by George Sinclair's mercenaries who'd served on the continent, influencing the development of the Scottish basket-hilted claymore.
**Pallasch - variation with same hilt and straight blade, but single-edged.

*Walloon Sword (épée wallone) - common in Germany, the Netherlands, Scandinavia, & Switzerland during the Thirty Years' War and Baroque era, the waloon sword sports an ambidextrous hilt with shell-guards and a knuckle-bow. Following their campaign in the Netherlands in 1672; when many of these German-made swords were captured from the Dutch; the French began producing this weapon as their first regulation sword. They were also used by the Swedish army as late as the 1850s.


Claymore (Scot Gaelic claidheamh mòr, "great sword" or "broad sword")
15th to 17th c. Scottish variant of the late medieval broadsword, characterized as having a cross hilt of forward sloping quillons with quatrefoil terminations, set with a wheel pommel and often capped by a crescent-shaped nut. Langets running down the centre of the blade from the guard. It averages around 55" (140 cm) in length, with a 13" (33 cm) grip, 42" (107 cm) blade, and weighing approx. 5.5 lb (2.5 kg). The last known battle that they saw significant use was the Battle of Killiecrankie.
Sword length - 47 to 55" (120 - 140 cm) Weight - avg. 5 to 6 lbs (2.2 - 2.8 kg)
Blade length - 39 to 45" (100 - 115 cm) 2.
*Claidheamh Dà Làimh (Scot Gaelic, "two-handed sword") - largest of the claymores, measuring up to 7' 6" (2.24 m) with a weight of up to 23 lbs (10 kg). One such sword was wielded by a 15th c. Scot giant of unknown name and origin, believed a member of the Maxwell clan.
*"Clamshell-hillted" Claymore - common variant with a crossguard of two downward-curving arms and two large, round, concave plates protecting the foregrip, for which it got its name.


Tsurugi
Japanese term for a broadsword akin to the Chinese jian, but larger.


Zweihänder (bidenhänder, schlachtschwerter, Ger. "two-hander")
16th c. German specialist weapon wielded by the Landsknechts' elite troops, the Doppelsöldners ("double soldiers", so named because they recieved double pay). They are typically at least 4'7" (1.4 m) long, with a weight over 4.5 lbs (2 kg), requiring two hands to wield. It is the ideal weapon for protecting standard-bearers as the wielder can fend off multiple attackers using moulinets. It isn't carried in a sheath, rather across the shoulder like a halberd.

*Flamberge (flambard, flammard, Ger. flammenschwert, lit. "flame sword") - variation of the zweihänder with a characteristically undulating style of blade, used in the 16th c. by the Landsknecht Doppelsöldners When parrying with a flamberge, unpleasant vibrations are transmitted into the attacker's blade, causing the blades to slow contact with each other, as additional friction was encountered with each blade.

Jutte (or jitte as I've sometimes seen it spelled) vs sai... they seem pretty similar, so why did the jutte come into being?

Shipyard Locked said:
Jutte (or jitte as I've sometimes seen it spelled) vs sai... they seem pretty similar, so why did the jutte come into being?
Click to expand...

The Jutte/Jitte were wooden weapons originally designed for palace guards and samurai law enforcement officers (okappiki/doshin) because metal blades weren't allowed in the Shogun's palace.

LONGSWORDS
Thrusting swords designed to pierce armour, with thin, often edgeless, blades, popular from 15th c.


Espada Ropera (Sp. lit. "dress sword", Fr. espee rapiere)
15th c. forerunner of the rapier, starting point of the 'light blade' lineage, distnguishing itself from the rapier in that the blade, though thin, could still be used to make effective cuts. Manufactured in Toledo, its blade is 31 to 51" (80 - 130 cm) long & 1.2 to 2" (3 - 5 cm) wide.


Estoc ("thrust or point')
Variation of the longsword designed for combat with opponents in mail or plate armour, with a stiff, long, and straight blade possessing no cutting edge, ony a sharp point. Blade cross-sections are square, triangular, shomboid, or flat hexagonal, and many varieties provide an extended, two-handed grip. Other variations feature finger rings, curved quillions, or mimic the zweihänder with a long ricasso and secondary guard of parrierhaken, but very few developed anything close to a basket hilt. This would be the prototype for most future longswords, from rapiers to épées .
Sword length - 46 to 62" (1.17 - 1.6 m) Weight - avg. 4 lbs (2 kg)
Blade length - 36 to 52" (.91 - 1.32 m)


Panzerstecher - German and East European two-handed or "hand-and-half"-hilted variation of the Estoc with a long, edgeless blade of square or tringular cross-section for penetrating armour, "hand-and-a-half" hilted. Later 16th and 17th c. variations, one-handed and used by cavalry, were known as koncerz.


Rapier (espada, spada, tuck, Ital., stocco)
A slender, sharply pointed sword, ideal for thrusting attacks, used mainly in Europe in the 16th and 17th c., typically weighing 2.2lb (1 kg), with a long and slender blade 39" (1 m) or more in length, 1" ((2.5 cm) or less in width, and terminating in a sharply-pointed tip.
*Bilbo (Basque, labana bizkaitarra; Spanish, daga vizcaina, "biscayne dagger) - 16th c. small, cup-hilted rapier featuring well-tempered and flexible blades, popular aboard ships (playing a role similar to the cutlass) and merchants.
*Claybeg (Scot Gaelic, claidheamh beag "small sword") - Scottish rapier similar to the bilbo, with a basket-hilt akin to that of the claidheamh caol.
*Pappenheimer Rapiers - basket-hilted rapiers used during the thirty-years war and Baroque era, similar in style to the contemporary walloon sword.
*Smallsword (court sword, dress sword, épée de cour) - 17th - 18th c. light, one-handed sword derived from the rapier, designed for thrusting, originating in France as predecessor of the épée. Its popularity spread quickly across Europe as status symbols and fashion accessories.
**Colichemarde - smallsword considered a descendant of the "transition rapier", which itself evolved from the rapier due to the demand for a lighter sword better suited to parrying. The cross section is hexegonical or diamond-shaped, as the colichemarde's blade features a wide forte, often with several fullers, which then tapers drastically to the point after the fullers end. This shape combines good thrusting characteristics with good parrying characteristics. Its lighter weight and superior balance compared to the rapier allows faster and more accurate movements of the blade. It was popular in the the 17th c. among the royal European courts and the officers of the French-Indian War, and George Washington is known to have carried one.
**Épée - modern dueling sword derived from the smallsword, similar to a foil but heavier (average 300 to 450 g, max. 770 g) with a 3-sided blade (in contrast to the foil and sabre which have rectangular cross-sections).
**Spada da Lato ("side-sword") - early 16th c. Italian sword resembling the rapier but with a broader and shorter blade.


Spadroon (épée anglaise)
Light sword with a straight blade of the cut and thrust type, popular amongst military and naval officers in the late 18th c. Hilts are of the beaded or "five-ball" type with a stirrup guard. The blade possesses a broad central fuller and a single edge, often with a false edge near the top.

NIHONTŌ
A general term for any traditonally-made Japanese sword used by the samurai class of feudal Japan.

Meibutsu ("noted swords") was a special designation given to sword masterpieces, listed in a compliation from the 18th c. called the Kyoho Meibutsucho.


Katana (samurai sword)
Nihontō characterized by its distinctive appearance: a curved, slender, single-edged blade, between 23 to 29" (60 - 73 cm) in length, with a circular or square guard and long grip to accommodate two hands. The rise in popularity of the katana among the samurai is believed due to the changing nature of close combat warfare during the Muromachi period. The quicker draw of the sword is well-suited to combat where victory depended on quick response times. Katana facilitated this further when worn thrust through a belt-like sash (obi) with the edge facing up so that, ideally, a samurai could draw the sword and strike at the enemy with a single motion. The pairing of the katana with a smaller sword, such as a wakizashi, is called the daishō. The daishō could only be worn by samurai, symbolic of their personal honour and social power.
*Guntō - military Katana produced after the Haitō Edict (1876) that forbade the carrying of swords in public except for daimyo, the military, and police.


Kodachi ("short sword")
Nihontō mounted in tachi style but with a length of less than 2' (60 cm).


Nagamaki (Jap., "long wrapping")
Type of nihontō with an extra-long handle, featuring a blade of 2' (61 cm) or more, and a handle (tsuka) roughly equal in length. The blade is single-edged, resembling a naginata blade, the main difference beingthat the nagamaki handle is wrapped with leather or silk cords in a criss-cross manner, as with those of katanas.


Ōdachi (Japanese, "large sword")
Nihontō characterized by its length and religious inscriptions on the tang. To qualify as an ōdachi a sword must have a blade length of over 36" (91 cm).
*Nodachi ("field sword") - type of ōdachi with a blade up to 4' (122 cm) long.


Tachi
Nihontō of the Koto period, preceding the development of the katana. The tachi averages 27 to 32.5" (70 - 80 cm) in length. Compared to a katana, a tachi is somewhat lighter, more curved, has a greater taper from hilt to point, and a smaller point area.
*Uchigatana - Nihontō descending from the tachi, from 60 to 70 cm in length and light so that it can be wielded one-handed. It grew in popularity as a companion to polearms.


Wakizashi (Jap., lit. "side-inserted sword")
Nihontō with a blade between 12 to 24" (30 - 60 cm), with wakizashi closer to the length of a katana referred to as o-wakizashi, and those closer to the length of a tantō referred to as ko-wakizashi.

SHORTSWORDS


Acinaces (Old Persian *akīnakah)
Short sword or long dagger developed in the first millenium BC in the eastern Mediterranean region, originally by either the Scythians or the Medes, but made famous by the Persians, and then rapidly spreading throughout the ancient world. The blade is typically 14 - 18 in (35-45 cm) in length and double-edged, the guard often lobed with the hilt resembling that of a bollock dagger, and the pommel was split or of the "antennae" type. A thrusting weapon normally worn on the belt on the right side, drawn suddenly with the blade facing downwards for surprise stabbing attacks.


Arming Sword (knight's sword, knightly sword)
11th to 16th c. European sword with a straight double-edged blade of 27 to 32" (69 - 81 cm) and a single-handed cruciform hilt. 'Brazil-nut' pommels were the most common from 1000 - 1200AD, with the 'wheel' pommel appearing in the 11th c. and predominating from the 13th to 15th c.
Sword length - avg. 35" (90 cm) Weight - avg. 2.4 lbs (1.1 kg)
Blade length - avg. 30" (75 cm)
*Bastard Sword - single-handed arming sword with a compound-hilt and an irregular-length blade, referred to as "something shorter than the longsword, and yet longer than a shortsword," A form of German arming sword with a bastard-style compound hilt was called a degen ("knight's sword") or reitschwert ("cavalry sword").
*Katzbalger - Renaissance-era German short arming sword, notable for its sturdy build and distinctive figure-8-shaped guard. 30 - 33" (75 - 85 cm) long and weighing 2 to 4 lbs (1 - 2 kg), it was the signature blade of the Landsknecht.


Balisword
An exceptionally large balisong knife, two folding hilts, 20" (51 cm) long, cover the 17" (43 cm) blade, with the standard length of an open sword around 37" (94 cm).


Butterfly Sword (Chi., húdié shuāng dāo)
Usually wielded in pairs, butterfly swords possess a single-edged blade roughly as long as a human forearm, with a small crossguard similar to that of a sai, which can be used to block or hook an opponent's weapon. In some versions the crossguard is enlarged, offering a second handhold, so that the swords can be manipulated in a manner akin to a pair of tonfa. The blade is only sharpened along half of its edge; from the middle of the blade to the tip; the rest of the blade left blunt to deliver non-lethal strikes and to block without damaging the sharpened edge. Pairs of butterfly swords are often carried side by side within the same scabbard, giving the appearance of a single weapon.


Chokutō (Jap., "straight sword")
Uncurved sword produced prior the the 10th c., its distinctive straight blade similar to ancient swords of the Three Kingdoms Period of China.


Curtana (Cortana, Courtain)
Ceremonial sword whose name is derived from the Latin curtus, 'shortened'.


Gladius
The primary sword of Ancient Roman footsoldiers, adopted from the 3rd c. BC onwards from similar swords weilded by the Celtiberians, until it was superceded in the 2nd c. AD by the Spatha. Afer which 'gladius' continued to be used in as a general term for sword. There are several variations of Gladii, though all types are strong-bladed, two-edged or cutting and chopping, and possess a tapered point for thrusting and stabbing. The hilt is knobbed and the grip (capulus) is often ornate, with ridges for the fingers. The owner's name is frequently engraved or punched on the blade.
*Gladius Hispaniensis - "Spanish sword".The earliest type of Gladius, as well as the largest and heaviest, copied from the Celtiberians and used primarily from 200 - 20 BC, with a pronounced leaf-shape in comparison to later types.
Sword length - 30 to 34" (75 - 85 cm) Blade width - 1.9" (5 cm)
Blade length - 23 to 27" (60 - 68 cm) Weight - 1.9 lbs (900 g) to 2.2 lbs (1 kg)
*Mainz - Named for the city that grew out of a permenent encampment of soldiers where it was manufactured, the Mainz variety ischaracterized by a slight waist running the length of the blade tapering to a long point.
Sword length - 26 to 28" (65 - 70 cm) Blade width - 2.75" (7 cm)
Blade length - 20 to 22" (50 - 55 cm) Weight - 1.76 lbs (800 g)
*Fulham - The conjunction point between Mainz and Pompeii style gladii, featuring a slightly more narrow blade, with the main difference the triangular tip.
Sword length - 26 to 28" (65 - 70 cm) Blade width - 2.36" (6 cm)
Blade length - 20 to 22" (50 - 55 cm) Weight - 1.5 lbs (700 g)
*Pompeii - The most popular variety, short with parallel cutting edges and a triangular tip. The Pompeii gradually got longer, these later versions referred to as semi-spathas.
Sword length - 24 to 26" (60 - 65 cm) Blade width - 1.97" (5 cm)
Blade length - 18 to 20" (45 - 50 cm) Weight - 1.5 lbs (700 g)
The Mainz and Pompeii types were the two most common and served side-by-side.

  • Apr 29, 2017
  • #31

Executioner's Sword
Specifically designed for the decapitation of criminals, they were intended for two-handed use but their typical length is that of a single-handed sword (2.5-3' or 80-90 cm) with a rounded tip. The quillions were quite short & mainly straight, with a pear-shaped or faceted pommel. Originating in the 16th c., they falling out of use suddenly in the early 18th c. Often decorated with symbolic designs, scenes of execution, torture, or the Crucifixion, combined with moralistic inscriptions, and outside of executions they some times served as symbols of judicial power.


Hook Sword (tiger hooks, Chi. fu tao; hut tou gou, "tiger-head-hook")
Exotic weapon with a blade similar to that of the jian, though thicker and unsharpened, with a prong or hook near the tip, and substantial guards, in the style of butterfly swords. Often used in pairs, the hook is used to trap or deflect other weapons. Also sometimes called qian kun ri yue dāo, lit. "Heavenly Sun and Moon sword".


Hunting Sword
Single-handed shortsword dating to the 12th c. but saw new use by European hunting parties from the 17th to 19th c. It has a straight, single-edged blade of no more than 25" (63.5 cm) in length, designed for finishing off game in liue of using a further shot.


Jian
Chinese double-edged straight sword with a blade 17.5 to 32" (45 - 80 cm) in length and a weight of 1.5 to 2 lbs (700 - 900 g), dating as far back as the 7th c BC. In Chinese folklore it is known as "The Gentleman of Weapons", considered one of the 4 major weapons of China, along with the staff (Gun), spear (Qiang), and the Dāo (sabre).


Nandāo
Chinese sword type used mostly in contemporary wushu excercizes and forms, its blade bearing a strong resemblance to the butterfly sword, the main differences being the size and the lack of the D-shaped knuckle guards. It features a large metal crossguard useful in deflecting blows and hooking an opponent's weapon, and the blade is single-edged. The northern variation, Beidāo, features a slightly curved blade.


Pata (patta)
17th c. Indian sword ranging in length from 10 - 44", with a gauntlet integrated as a handguard, used in two-weapon fighting techniques called dand patta.


Spatha (Greek spáthās, "broad blade")
Long and straight one-handed sword, measuring 30 - 39" (.75 - 1 m), employed through-out Europe in the 1st millennium AD and in the Roman Empire territories untl 600 AD, gradually replacing the gladius as the standard heavy infantry weapon. The infantry version features a long point, a variation caried by the cavalry had a rounded tip to prevent accidental stabbing of the feet. The spatha was used extensively by Germanic warriors, and popular in Britain, possibly evolving into the knightly sword of the High Middle Ages. In the Byzantine empire, spatharios ("bearer of the spatha") was a mid-level court title, variants deriving from it including protospatharios, spatharokandidatos, and, reserved for eunuchs, spatharokoubikoularios.
*Longbard Spatha - Later derivative used by the Vikings from 600 to 1000 AD, with a more acute distal taper and point, and deep fullers running along its length. Its single-handed hilt sported a unique-shaped pommel, flat the side of the grip roughly triangular, with the flat curving later on to fit the hand. Sometimes called "the Viking Sword", it actually had widespread popularity all over continental Europe from 700 to 1000AD.Many of the best blades were Frankish in origin, with significantly better balance.
*Norman Spatha - Evolving from the Longbard Spatha, during Norman times the blade tapered slightly less and increased in length some 3.9" (100mm). Instead of the 'Brazil-nut" pommel, a thick disc-shaped pommel was attached "on-edge" to the bottom of an iron hilt, while the upper guard grew substantially.


Xiphos ("penetrating light")
Single-handed double-edged sword used as a secondary battlefield weapon after the spear or javelin by the ancient Greeks. The blade is 'leaf-shaped', generally 50 - 60 cm long, widest at about 2/3 of its length, and ending in a long point. Originally made of bronze as the metal is soft and easy to form into the leaf-shape when cast, by the classical period they were forged of iron. The swords of the La Tène-era Celts were of a virtually identical design.
*Spartan Xiphos - variation only 30cm long used during the Greco-Persian wars.

Panzerstecher just means something like "armor stabber" and there are two kinds of weapons.
The Panzerstecher sword is an Estoc-like weapon.
The Panzerstecher dagger is a Rondel-like weapon.
Your text describes the Estoc sword version, your picture is of the Rondel dagger version.

If anyone has a chance, I recommend watching Forged in Fire, a blade-smithing competition. The final challenge involves creating a historically accurate weapon and they often test them on ballistic dummies and armor, and it was an eye opener for me just how much damage a lot of even "unimpressive" weapons can do.
